About this work

Experiences built to move people

Over two years at Intuit, I owned end-to-end experience design for the Marketing & Communications organization — a team of 800+ professionals across PMM, Brand, Creative, and Ops. My work spanned quarterly live and virtual events, vendor-produced physical environments, multi-track programming, and personalized physical touchpoints mailed to attendees worldwide.


I work with production partners to design spaces, with makers and small businesses to create tactile moments, and with cross-functional teams to hold quality across every format at scale.

Case Study 01 Hybrid · In-Person + Virtual

Marketing AI
Hackathon

Intuit Marketing's inaugural AI Hackathon — a full-day maker experience for 143 marketers across two build tracks, designed so in-person and virtual attendees had equally rich, equally personal experiences.

Attendees — 84% of addressable PMM + Brand audience
14
AI coaches, rotating across tracks all day
5
Executive sponsors who stayed the full event
3
Production-grade AI prototypes built in <2 hours

Judging Criteria
Experience Arc
🌱
Prework
Use case submissions, team sign-ups, data readiness checks — 4 weeks before
🔨
Hackathon Day
Two tracks: AI Assistants & Agentic Workflows. 2.5 hr build, gallery walk, swag distribution
🔁
2-Week Sprint
Coach office hours, iteration labs, team syncs to refine prototypes for showcase
🏆
Showcase + Awards
Virtual ceremony, peer voting via Kahoot, $500 Spotlight prizes + trophies
📦
Lego Delivery
Custom archetype figures + farmers market goods shipped to all virtual attendees
Signature Design Detail

The Lego
Archetype System

At the close of the Hackathon, every team spent 5 minutes doing something unusual: nominating each other for an archetype based on how they'd contributed to the build that day. Not personality — impact.

Each person received a custom-built Lego figure representing their archetype at registration. Virtual attendees had theirs shipped alongside their farmers market goods. It was the moment that made the experience personal, tangible, and worth talking about.

Design Philosophy

How I think about
experience

01

Physical & digital are one experience

I design the farmers market setup and the virtual chat channel with equal care. The swag that ships to remote attendees isn't an afterthought — it's the bridge that makes hybrid feel genuinely equitable.

02

The closing ritual matters most

Most events end with a survey link. I end with a Lego figure. The last 10 minutes of an experience determines how people remember the whole thing — so I design the close with as much intentionality as the open.

03

Personalization at scale is a design problem

143 custom Lego archetypes. 500 curated event bags. Multi-track programming that lets every person build their own agenda. Scale doesn't require generic — it requires systems thinking applied to moments that feel individual.

04

Quality standards travel without me

I write playbooks so the experience doesn't degrade when I'm not in the room. Every event I design produces documentation that lets the next producer hold the same bar.

05

I prototype before I produce

Before committing to a build, I test it. Two competing microsite prototypes, 5 user interviews, a hypothesis before every major format decision. I don't ship at scale what I haven't validated at small scale.

06

Vendors are creative partners

Bluegoose, the chocolate makers, the cotton candy vendor — I treat production partners as collaborators with creative input, not just logistics resources. That's how the farmers market became a farmers market and not just a table with snacks.

something worth feeling.

I'm pursuing roles in experience design, learning strategy, and AI adoption & enablement — where the mandate is to take something that works at small scale and build the systems, curriculum, and culture to make it work at enterprise scale.
